convo.text: Should we use a monorepo or multiple repos for a 20-engineer startup? Give me a recommendation and your reasoning.

**Recommendation: Use a monorepo.** For a 20-engineer startup, this is the right default in the large majority of cases. Below is my reasoning, plus the honest exceptions.

## Why monorepo wins at your scale

**1. Atomic cross-cutting changes.** At 20 engineers you almost certainly have shared code (types, API contracts, UI components, utils). In a monorepo, a change to a shared library and all its consumers lands in *one* PR/commit — you can never ship a breaking change that leaves another service on the old version. With multi-repo you get version skew, "which repo do I bump first," and a coordination tax that grows faster than your headcount.

**2. One source of truth for tooling.** Single lint/format/CI config, one dependency version policy, one place to upgrade the framework. Multi-repo means N copies of CI that drift apart, and "the auth lib is on v2 here but v1 there" becomes a recurring fire.

**3. Discoverability and refactoring.** Anyone can grep the whole codebase, find every caller of a function, and refactor with confidence. This is huge for a small team where people wear multiple hats and ownership boundaries are still fluid.

**4. Lower operational overhead.** 20 engineers can't afford a dedicated platform team. Multi-repo's hidden cost is the glue: shared-library publishing pipelines, version bumping, dependency dashboards, repo-creation boilerplate. A monorepo collapses most of that.

**5. Your boundaries are still wrong.** At 20 people, your service/module boundaries *will* change. Monorepo lets you move a boundary by moving a folder. Multi-repo bakes today's (probably wrong) architecture into hard-to-reverse repo splits.

## The real cost of monorepo (and why it's manageable now)

The classic monorepo objections only bite at much larger scale:
- **Slow CI / builds** → solved by affected-only builds (Nx, Turborepo, Bazel). At 20 engineers a tool like Turborepo/Nx is plenty; you don't need Bazel yet.
- **Git performance** → not a problem until hundreds of engineers / huge binary history.
- **Access control** → if you need to silo a small group, that's the main legitimate reason to peel a repo out (see below).

So adopt a build tool with caching/affected-graph support from the start, and the headline downside mostly disappears.

## When I'd deviate (the honest exceptions)

Split a repo out when one of these is genuinely true — not preemptively:
- **Hard security/compliance isolation** — e.g. a payments or crypto-signing component that a subset of engineers should access. Access control is repo-granular in most hosts.
- **Open-source / SDK you publish** — a client SDK that lives a public, independently-versioned life belongs in its own repo.
- **Truly independent product line** with its own team, release cadence, and zero shared code.
- **Third-party / contractor scoping** where you can't give outside developers the whole tree.

## Bottom line

Default to a **single monorepo** with a caching-aware build tool (Turborepo or Nx) and clear folder-level ownership (CODEOWNERS). Peel off individual repos *only* for the specific isolation/publishing reasons above. You can always split later — splitting a folder into its own repo is far easier than merging diverged repos back together, so the monorepo is the lower-regret starting point.

One caveat worth flagging: the recommendation leans on you having meaningful shared code and overlapping ownership. If your "20 engineers" are actually two fully independent products with separate teams and no shared code, tell me and I'll adjust — that's the one shape where two repos can be cleaner from day one.

**Recommendation: Use a monorepo.** For a 20-engineer startup this is the right call, and I'd commit to it as a definitive default rather than something to revisit.

## Why monorepo wins at your scale

**1. Atomic cross-cutting changes.** At 20 engineers you have shared code (types, API contracts, UI components, utils). In a monorepo, a change to a shared library and all its consumers lands in *one* PR/commit — you can never ship a breaking change that leaves another service on the old version. With multi-repo you get version skew, "which repo do I bump first," and a coordination tax that grows faster than headcount.

**2. One source of truth for tooling.** Single lint/format/CI config, one dependency version policy, one place to upgrade the framework. Multi-repo means N copies of CI that drift apart, and "the auth lib is on v2 here but v1 there" becomes a recurring fire.

**3. Discoverability and refactoring.** Anyone can grep the whole codebase, find every caller of a function, and refactor with confidence. This is huge for a small team where people wear multiple hats and ownership boundaries are still fluid.

**4. Lower operational overhead.** 20 engineers can't afford a dedicated platform team. Multi-repo's hidden cost is the glue: shared-library publishing pipelines, version bumping, dependency dashboards, repo-creation boilerplate. A monorepo collapses most of that.

**5. Your boundaries are still wrong.** At 20 people, your service/module boundaries *will* change. Monorepo lets you move a boundary by moving a folder. Multi-repo bakes today's (probably wrong) architecture into hard-to-reverse repo splits.

## The real cost of monorepo (and why it's manageable now)

The classic monorepo objections only bite at much larger scale:
- **Slow CI / builds** → solved by affected-only builds. At 20 engineers, Turborepo or Nx is plenty; you don't need Bazel yet.
- **Git performance** → not a problem until hundreds of engineers / huge binary history.
- **Access control** → repo-granular on most hosts; this is the one legitimate reason to peel a repo out (below).

Adopt a build tool with caching/affected-graph support from day one and the headline downside mostly disappears.

## The exceptions — peel out a single repo, don't go multi-repo

Even in the cases below, the answer is "monorepo *plus* one carved-out repo," not "abandon the monorepo." Split a repo out only when one of these is genuinely true:
- **Hard security/compliance isolation** — e.g. a payments or crypto-signing component a subset of engineers should access.
- **Open-source / SDK you publish** — a client SDK with an independent public version lives in its own repo.
- **Truly independent product line** with its own team, release cadence, and zero shared code.
- **Third-party / contractor scoping** where you can't expose the whole tree.

The one shape where two repos can be cleaner from day one is if your "20 engineers" are actually two fully independent products with separate teams and no shared code. Even then I'd lean monorepo for the tooling and refactoring wins — but two repos is defensible, and that's the *only* configuration where I wouldn't push back on it.

## Bottom line

Go with a **single monorepo** using Turborepo or Nx and folder-level ownership via CODEOWNERS. Carve out an individual repo only for the specific isolation/publishing reasons above. Splitting a folder into its own repo later is easy; merging diverged repos back together is painful — so the monorepo is the lower-regret starting point, and it's the one I'd ship.
Should we use a monorepo or multiple repos for a 20-engineer startup? Give me a recommendation and your reasoning.

Use a monorepo. For a 20-engineer startup, that's the default I'd recommend unless you have a specific reason that rules it out (I'll cover those at the end).

**Why a monorepo fits your stage**

At 20 engineers you're big enough to feel cross-repo coordination pain but small enough that the monorepo's costs are still cheap to manage. The things that actually matter day-to-day:

- **Atomic changes across boundaries.** When someone changes an API and the three services that call it, that's one pull request, one review, one merge. In a multi-repo world it's four PRs that have to land in the right order, plus a window where things are half-migrated. At your size you'll be doing this kind of cross-cutting change constantly because the architecture is still moving.
- **No internal versioning tax.** Shared libraries are just imported from source. You skip the whole cycle of publishing a package, bumping a version, and updating consumers — which is real overhead that buys you almost nothing when all the consumers are your own team.
- **One way to do everything.** A single lint config, test runner, CI setup, and dependency set. New hires clone one thing and are productive. You avoid the slow drift where each repo grows its own slightly-different tooling that nobody has time to reconcile.
- **Refactoring stays cheap.** Renaming something, moving code, or finding every caller is a single search-and-replace with the compiler checking you. This is the capability you lose first and miss most in a polyrepo, and it's exactly what an early-stage codebase needs while it's still finding its shape.

**The real costs, and why they're manageable now**

- CI gets slower as the repo grows — you'll eventually need build caching and "only test what changed" (tools like Turborepo, Nx, or Bazel). You probably don't need that on day one, but budget for one engineer to own build/CI tooling before it becomes painful, likely well before you hit 50 people.
- Git operations and IDE indexing slow down at very large scale — not a concern at 20 engineers, possibly years away.
- Fine-grained access control is weaker (everyone tends to see everything). Usually fine for a startup; a blocker only if you have hard compliance walls between teams.

**When I'd reconsider**

Pick multiple repos if one of these is true: you have genuinely independent products with separate teams and almost no shared code; you have strict regulatory isolation requirements; or you're publishing open-source/SDK artifacts that need their own lifecycle (those can live in their own repos even if everything else is a monorepo — a hybrid is fine).

Net: start with a monorepo, and plan to invest in build tooling as the one cost you'll actually have to pay for it. It's far easier to split a monorepo later than to stitch scattered repos back together.
